title: Level 12: SPI Comm (Master Mode)
aim: To execute high-speed bidirectional communication by configuring the ATmega328P as an SPI Master.
objective: Understand the Serial Peripheral Interface (SPI) pins (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS), configure SPCR (Control Register), and transmit data using SPSR and SPDR.
theory:
SPI Architecture

SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface) is a synchronous, full-duplex protocol. It is vastly faster than UART (up to 8 Megabits per second). It uses 4 dedicated hardware pins:
MOSI: Master Out Slave In.
MISO: Master In Slave Out.
SCK: Serial Clock generated ONLY by the Master.
SS: Slave Select, driven LOW by the Master to tell a specific component on the bus to listen.

Full Duplex Exchanging

In SPI, reading and writing happen simultaneously. As the master shifts an 8-bit command bit-by-bit out of MOSI, the slave device shifts an 8-bit response bit-by-bit into MISO via shift registers. To read a byte from a slave, you MUST transmit a dummy byte (like 0xFF) just to generate the 8 clock pulses on SCK to suck the answer in.

Configuring the SPCR

The Master MUST explicitly set the MOSI, SCK, and SS pins as OUTPUTs in DDRB. The MISO pin must be an INPUT. The SPI Control Register (SPCR) enables SPI (SPE=1) and explicitly asserts Master mode (MSTR=1).

pretest.question: Why must the ATmega328P (as Master) explicitly set the SS (Slave Select) pin to OUTPUT and keep it HIGH if it’s not communicating, even if it uses another pin to select the slave?
options: If SS is an input and pulled LOW externally, the hardware violently forces the ATmega328P into Slave Mode, destroying Master operations
To tell the UART hardware to turn off
Because the SPCR register requires it for SPI clock division
pretest.explanation: This is a critical hardware feature. If the physical SS pin (PB2) is configured as input and drops to LOW, it is an over-ride that disables the MSTR bit in SPCR.

pretest.question: What is the primary purpose of the DDRx register in the ATmega328P?
options: To configure pins as input or output
pretest.explanation: DDR configures the direction of each GPIO pin.

pretest.question: Which header file provides basic I/O register definitions for AVR?
pretest.explanation: <avr/io.h> provides register definitions for all AVR peripherals.

pretest.question: What does the volatile keyword tell the C compiler?
options: May change unexpectedly, so do not optimize reads
pretest.explanation: volatile prevents the compiler from caching the variable.

pretest.question: How many GPIO pins does the ATmega328P have in total?
pretest.explanation: 23 GPIO pins across Port B, C, and D.

pretest.question: What is the maximum clock frequency of the ATmega328P?
pretest.explanation: Supports up to 20MHz, though Arduino UNO uses 16MHz.

pretest.question: What is the purpose of sei()?
pretest.explanation: sei() sets the global interrupt enable flag in SREG.

pretest.question: Which AVR memory type retains data after power-off?
What is the width of each ATmega328P port register?
pretest.explanation: The ATmega328P is an 8-bit MCU; all port registers are 8 bits wide.

pretest.question: What is bit manipulation?
options: Operating on individual bits using bitwise operators
pretest.explanation: Bit manipulation uses AND, OR, XOR, and SHIFT.

procedure: Configure DDRB explicitly. Set MOSI (PB3), SCK (PB5), and SS (PB2) as outputs. Keep MISO (PB4) as an input.
Drive SS High (PORTB |= (1<<2)) so no slaves are falsely selected yet.
Initialize the SPI Control Register (SPCR). Enable SPI (SPE=1), Master (MSTR=1), and optionally set the clock rate to fosc/16 (SPR0=1).
To transmit, pull the target slave's chip select pin LOW.
Load data into the SPI Data Register: `SPDR = data;`
Wait for the SPI Interrupt Flag (SPIF) in the SPSR register to be set to 1.
The received answer from the slave is now living inside `SPDR`. Read it, then pull the chip select pin HIGH to end the transaction.

posttest.question: When loading data into SPDR, what action generates the serial clock pulses (SCK) to move the bits?
options: The act of writing to SPDR physically triggers the 8-pulse clock hardware sequence
posttest.explanation: Writing an 8-bit value directly into SPDR immediately and automatically kicks off the 8 hardware SCK pulses required to execute the transaction.

posttest.question: Why is it good practice to use #define for pin numbers?
posttest.explanation: #define creates named constants for self-documenting code.

posttest.question: What tool converts C source to .hex for AVR?
posttest.explanation: avr-gcc compiles C to AVR machine code.

posttest.question: What is the difference between PORTB and PINB?
options: PORTB writes output; PINB reads actual pin voltages
posttest.explanation: PORTB drives outputs; PINB reads real-time pin states.

posttest.question: What happens to SRAM when power is removed?
What is the stack used for in AVR?
posttest.explanation: Stack stores return addresses and local variables.

posttest.question: Which is NOT a valid ATmega328P port?
posttest.explanation: ATmega328P has only Port B, C, and D. No Port A.

posttest.question: What is a watchdog timer used for?
posttest.explanation: WDT auto-resets MCU when not periodically fed.

posttest.question: What does the SREG register contain?
posttest.explanation: SREG holds CPU flags and the global interrupt enable bit.

posttest.question: In an ISR, interrupts are automatically:
posttest.explanation: AVR clears I-flag in SREG upon entering an ISR.

feedback: You've mastered synchronous protocols. SPI is extensively used for addressing high-speed hardware: SD Cards, TFT Screens, and Flash Memory chips.
